title: 5 roles to automate before you hire for them
excerpt: If you're about to post a job listing for any of these roles, pause for a second. AI might be a better fit — and a faster one.
author: Simon Insinger
content:
## Where to start

Every company thinking about AI automation asks the same thing: which role first?

After building AI employees for a range of businesses, we've seen clear patterns in what works best right away.

## 1. Executive assistant

EAs spend most of their time on tasks that follow rules. Calendar management, email sorting, meeting prep, travel booking — it's structured work.

Typical savings: $60K-90K/year in salary and benefits
Time to see results: about 30 days

This is our most common build. It makes the founder's life better immediately, which tends to sell the concept internally.

## 2. Sales development rep

SDR work comes down to volume and personalization. Researching prospects, writing outreach, managing follow-up sequences — AI handles all of this at a scale that one person can't match.

Typical savings: $65K-85K/year per SDR
Time to see results: about 45 days

Most of our SDR clients don't just save money — they actually book more meetings. The AI doesn't get tired or skip follow-ups.

## 3. Research analyst

Research is reading, filtering, and summarizing. AI processes information much faster than a person and can pull from dozens of sources at once.

Typical savings: $70K-100K/year per analyst
Time to see results: about 30 days

Investment firms, strategy teams, and competitive intelligence functions get the most out of this one.

## 4. Tier 1 customer support

Most support tickets follow predictable patterns. AI can resolve 60-80% of them instantly and pass the rest to a human agent.

Typical savings: $40K-55K/year per agent
Time to see results: about 14 days

The fastest payback on this list. Response times go from hours to seconds, which customers tend to appreciate.

## 5. Data entry and operations coordination

The most obviously automatable role on the list. CRM updates, cross-system data entry, routine coordination — yet plenty of companies still have humans doing this full-time.

Typical savings: $45K-60K/year
Time to see results: about 14 days

Eliminates data entry errors and frees up your ops people for work that requires thinking.

## Picking your first one

Start with the role that costs the most relative to the work it produces, has the clearest repeatable workflows, and where 24/7 availability would help. For most founders, that's the EA. But every business is different.

## What happens after the first one

Once the first AI employee is running, you start seeing automation opportunities you missed before. The second build goes faster because you've done it once. The third goes even faster.

It snowballs, in a good way.
